title: Lamb tagine with apricots and chickpeas
subtitle: Slow-braised lamb shoulder with warm spices, dried apricots, and preserved lemon
excerpt: Chunks of lamb shoulder braised with ras-el-hanout, saffron, and ginger in a tagine or dutch oven until very tender, with dried apricots for sweetness, chickpeas for body, and a strip of preserved lemon for sharpness. Served over couscous. The sauce should be rich and fragrant rather than watery.
sourceNotes: Lamb tagine with fruit and spice is a classic of Moroccan cuisine, described in the fourteenth-century Andalusian cookery text Manuscrito Anónimo and in nineteenth-century Moroccan household cookery accounts. The combination of savoury meat, dried fruit, and warm spice is the defining flavour profile of the Moroccan kitchen.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the couscous. Defrost overnight and reheat gently, stirring in the preserved lemon fresh.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tagine improves the next day. Reheat gently with a splash of water over medium-low heat.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan 140°C; or simmer on the hob in a dutch oven

content.text: A tagine is both a vessel and a dish. The conical clay lid traps steam, condenses it, and returns it to the pot, keeping the meat moist over a long low cook. The dutch oven does the same job and is more practical for most kitchens. Either way, the technique is the same: soften the aromatics, brown the meat, build the spiced sauce, and leave it undisturbed over the lowest possible heat for 90 minutes to 2 hours.

Ras-el-hanout is a Moroccan spice blend that varies by seller and region but typically contains cumin, coriander, ginger, cinnamon, turmeric, black pepper, and often rose petals and other florals. Using a good ras-el-hanout is more effective than trying to replicate it from scratch. The preserved lemon goes in at the end — it loses its distinctive floral-salt quality if added too early.

items.prepNote: soaked in 2 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes
preserved, rind finely sliced, flesh discarded

content.text: Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a dutch oven or heavy-based casserole over high heat. Season the {{lamb-shoulder}} of lamb with the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and brown in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deep golden all over. Set aside. Reduce the heat to medium.

Add the {{onion}} onions to the pot and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 2 more minutes. Add the {{ras-el-hanout}} of ras-el-hanout and {{ginger-ground}} of ginger and stir for 1 minute. Return the lamb to the pot. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of stock and the saffron water. Add the {{dried-apricots}} of apricots, {{chickpeas-tinned}} of chickpeas, and {{honey}} of honey. Stir to combine. Bring to a simmer.

Cover and cook over very low heat for 1 hour 30 minutes, or transfer to the oven at 160°C for the same time. The lamb should be completely tender and pulling apart. Remove the lid and simmer uncovered for 15 minutes to thicken the sauce. Stir in the sliced rind of the {{lemon}} preserved lemon. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Serve over couscous with {{coriander}} of coriander leaves scattered over.

items.cause: Too much stock or lid not removed at the end
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for 20 to 25 minutes to reduce; the sauce should coat the meat rather than pool on the plate

items.symptom: Lamb is tough after the full cook time
items.cause: Heat too high or lamb cut too small, causing it to dry out before fully braising
items.fix: Cut chunks no smaller than 4 cm; ensure a gentle simmer with the lid on; add 30 more minutes if needed

items.symptom: Preserved lemon taste is too sharp
items.cause: Added too early and overcooked, or too much used
items.fix: Add the preserved lemon rind only at the end, off the heat; taste before adding and use a small amount first

content.text: The tagine is the defining cooking vessel of Morocco and much of North Africa. Its shape (a shallow earthenware base and a pointed conical lid) is practical: the cone collects steam and channels it back to the base, effectively self-basting over a long low cook without the need for much liquid. The braising style found in Moroccan tagines (meat, dried fruit, spice, a little sweetness, a little sourness) descends from medieval Arab-Andalusian culinary traditions. The preserved lemon is a Moroccan flavour unlike anything in European cooking.
